sentences.text: You want me to continue from there or go--
From there.
Some months later, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I received a letter from her uncle.
And he had been found.
He was living in Adelaide.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he said that he had some friends in Haifa, which was in Palestine.
And if I was able to get her there, he would make sure that his friends would take care of her from there.
And at that time, the-- there were organizations working in France and Belgium, enabling Jewish refugees to get down to Marseilles, and ultimately to get to Palestine.
And we got her on to one of these groups.
And she did get down to Marseilles.
And eventually, she did arrive in Haifa.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And about a year later-- it was almost a year later-- that I received a letter, which actually went to my home in London.
Because I had given them my home address, fearing that by the time I might have been out of service, that he wrote back.
And the letter eventually came back to me that she did.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She made [INAUDIBLE].
We corresponded for a little while.
And then after a while, it just disappeared.
And that's the-- that was Sophie.
Yes.
I would say probably about 10 to 14 days, perhaps even more.
I can't remember the exact date.
Yes.
By the British military.
No.
They weren't.
They were Wehrmacht.
They were army.
From what I was given to understand, the SS had gone by that time.
And that the-- they weren't only German soldiers.
They were soldiers from other countries that were in German uniforms, from Romania and other eastern European countries.
No.
The general attitude [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from people working in the camp and other Germans that I'd met outside the camp-- they said they didn't know what was going on.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We asked them whether they had noses that they could smell.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But the-- going back to the camp, no.
They were working like automats.
They just did as they were told.
I think the shock of what they had been involved in was now become accepted.
And they were just working like automats-- just doing what they were told.
[SIGHS] I can't remember.
But I'm sure the records will show that.
I know the first unit to go in was the 8th Armored Brigade.
A lot has been done, yes.
A medical unit had been set up.
A hospital ward had been set up.
A cleansing station had been set up.
Typhus was absolutely rife.
Yes.
We were discouraged from going there.
We saw them from a distance.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The bulldozers were working, pushing all the piles of bodies into this huge pit.
And the work was overseen-- was overseen by the British military, who supervised it.
There was a Jewish [INAUDIBLE] in the camp at the time, a Reverend [INAUDIBLE] I didn't meet him personally.
But I knew that he was there.
And at a later stage, another [INAUDIBLE] came along-- a Dr.
Levy, who was-- I didn't meet him in the camp.
I did meet him in Brussels at a later stage.
And I had met him several times since then.
Yes.
Although, I wasn't there personally, I do-- I do know that services did take place.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
There were clergy, yes, of course.
I think it was difficult to sort out who was what.
Two.
It was bad, really bad.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were three tiers of bunks.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The bodies were piled high.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was staggering.
It was unbelievable.
But it was true.
It was there.
The eyes don't lie.
We just went to two barracks.
[CRYING] Though, because we were not actually part of the working parties there, we were just literally visitors, we were discouraged from getting too involved.
We-- we were given jobs unloading food stores.
Although, the-- most of them were starving, there were warehouses full of food.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But by that time, it was too late to some of them.
Yes.
Some of them had Red Cross markings on them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think they were intended for the prisoners.
Yes.
No.
If they were, they didn't show signs of life.
I just thought they were just shown there and left to die.
Hardly any.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't see any sanitation apart from a couple of buckets.
But I don't think I was looking at that at that particular time.
We were just mesmerized by the-- what we were seeing.
Not that I could see.
But the weather at that time was-- if I remember correctly-- was quite warm, which made-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] flies were very evident there.
It's hot.
Yeah.
Lots of flies there.
I didn't see any.
But there could have been.
I didn't see any.
Adjacent to the gate of the camp was a-- was the camp's commandant office, which is where we unloaded our radio equipment.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were some other buildings in the distance which looked a bit more sophisticated, more modern.
But we weren't allowed to go there.
We were given to understand that some German officers were being interrogated there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we did ultimately find out, of course, that the camp's commander's name was Kramer.
No.
I'm sure he was taken away and ultimately dealt with by the authorities.
Yes.
Well, there were some civilians that had come out from England, medical people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were wearing white coats and arm bands.
And we assume, yeah, they were English.
Yes.
Yes.
They had definitely come out from England.
Yes.
I didn't see any.
No.
No, I didn't see-- I didn't see any.
No.
But some of the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] prisoners could have been.
But they were so emaciated and starving.
They were stooped.
They could've been quite young.
Although, I don't think the word children would apply.
I don't think so.
They weren't.
Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] those that could, who were in a condition to receive treatment, were being stretchered.
And they were being taken to medical tents which had been set up and to buildings which had been sanitized.
And to those who were in a condition to receive treatment, they were being looked after as best as was available.
Yes.
And the fresh water tanks were being brought in by the army.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] No.
I didn't see any children.
It was away over on the other side of the camp.
We could see the markings on the building.
But we didn't actually get near there.
No.
But I do know that they were fully occupied with typhus.
That was the big number one problem.
It was absolutely rife there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The weather was warm.
I remember it was warm.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] many-- there were many bodies laying around.
It was-- it was so-- it was thousands and thousands of bodies all over the place.
And I suppose even the people who were working there, there was a limit to what even they could do.
And a lot of the bodies just still laid there.
The flies the general-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the residue of a dead body laying there, laying around, it was quite unpleasant.
Oh, yes.
We were not told what to expect.
[SIGHS] The shock-- we didn't have a chance to think, you know.
It was just absolute shock, you know.
It-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was absolute hell, you know.
We just couldn't believe it was happening.
Myself and my colleagues, we're just young-- young men from a background where we'd never expected or seen this sort of thing.
Some of us were just about 23 of 24.
One of our youngsters was only 18.
He collapsed.
No.
Well, no.
I'm sure they weren't.
No, no.
Because I was still the only Jew in my unit, even at that time.
Pardon?
Oh, they were shocked, even as people.
I mean, nobody could fail to be affected by what they had seen.
No.
I didn't see any.
No.
They were there.
But we had no means of conversation.
They were there.
Where were they?
Working under military supervision.
Clearing.
Clearing out the-- the bodies, moving those who could receive attention.
And a very serious attempt was made to clean up the whole area.
[INAUDIBLE] those who were able to walk and receive attention.
No.
We were-- as we were not part of the camp's personnel, we were discouraged from taking a personal part.
Because the authorities knew that we were only there for a matter of hours.
In actual fact, we stayed there two days.
But as we were not part of the occupying units, we were kept separate.
No.
We didn't have an officer with us.
We just had a corporal.
And the rest of us were just privates.
No.
No.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Did you take any photos or memorabilia from the camp?
No.
There were 20 on my truck.
Yeah.
No.
I can only assume that they had been in the camp a relatively short time.
Perhaps they had been transferred from other the camps.
They appeared to be in a better condition than the average prisoner.
Their clothing was relatively better.
Some of them had small bundles of belongings.
And I assume the authorities felt that they were better off elsewhere than remaining in the camp.
I'm also quite sure that some arrangements were made with the authorities in Brussels.
Because when we arrived there, all the civilian people were waiting for us to arrive.
No.
I can-- I can only assume it was either through the Red Cross or some similar organization.
But it was certainly prearranged.
From the camp?
Well--
Well, I was sitting up front.
Although, I was able to lean back and see them.
In the beginning, they were absolutely quiet, totally shocked, speechless.
But as the journey progressed and we issued some rations that we'd given them, they became-- they became more talkative.
And many of them were speaking in their own language.
It was not Yiddish.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It could have been German, Polish, or whatever.
I couldn't identify it.
That's one of those reasons why we just couldn't communicate with them.
[SIGHS] Because of the conditions and the privations that they had suffered, it was difficult to be accurate.
But it could have been anything from 16 to 30 or 40.
I don't think so.
They were all women-- could have been related, but I was-- I couldn't be sure of that.
Five to six hours.
There were civilians waiting there in the square outside the [INAUDIBLE] station.
Yes.
There were military personnel there, yes.
The members of the medical corps were there.
And there were also some people wearing the Red Cross arm band, which made me to understand that arrangements had-- had been-- had been made.
It was six trucks and approximately 20 to each truck.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But once they had been dispersed, I lost contact with them.

I can only assume that once a member of the civilians who were there who had taken one of the prisoners under their care-- there could-- I would most probably assume there was some kind of registration to keep track of them.
Because I had the papers of the women on my truck, which I handed over to an official.
And I assume that the other truck drivers had the same.
I reported back to my unit, yes.
Cleaned up, reported back for duty.
And we discussed with our colleagues what we had seen and where we had been.
There was no restriction.
A lot of our people were keen to hear what had happened and where we had been.
And no, there was no restriction on talking about it, absolutely not, no, no.
Yes.
I remained with my unit for the rest of 1945.
I came home on leave in September of that year, my first time in two years.
And while I was home, it was V-E Day.
I remember that quite clearly, because I think my son was conceived that night.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had extra leave.
And it was great.
The war was over, both in Europe and in-- in the Far East.
But I still had to return to my unit, even though the war had finished.
And I was ultimately-- I went back to Brussels to work on routine communications, routine duties.
And I was ultimately demobilized on the 1st of January 1946, after five and a half years in the army.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I returned to civilian life, which is extremely difficult.
I mean settling down after that, it was-- I was very-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was difficult to get along with.
By that time, my wife and I had a home.
We had a flat in a house in South London.
And it took quite a long time for me to settle down to what was a normal civilian life.
Oh, yes.
Yes.
[INAUDIBLE]
No.
I didn't have nightmares.
But we talked a lot.

A lot of people and Jewish organizations, and even non-Jewish organizations, once-- once troops began to come back who had been involved in Germany, they wanted to hear first-hand information as to what really went on.
Bergen-Belsen was the only concentration camp in the British sector, which was-- Bergen-Belsen was near Hanover in North Germany.
Most of the other concentration camps were either in-- mostly, were in the Russian or the American sectors, like Buchenwald and Auschwitz and all those other places.
But Bergen-Belsen was the only camp in the British sector.
And lots of people wanted to hear about it.
And as time went by, I I felt more rational, that I could talk about it and-- and in more detail.
I must confess, I lost part of it.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Although, my family was orthodox, as the years progressed, I felt that I was more secular than orthodox.
And all kinds of questions were being made by Anglo-jury as to why it happened.
Where was God?
And things like that-- and I had very mixed feelings as to how, why, what, and when.
But without wishing to offend those of the more orthodox kind, I have to admit that my faith in God had diminished somewhat.
Perhaps, my views were unfounded, but that's the way I felt at the time.
And-- and from then on, once I became more secular in my attitude, I'm still a Jew.
I recognize that I'm a Jew.
I observe the customs.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I go to shul on various occasions.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that's as far as it went.
I had no reason to regret my previous life.
I was still a relatively young man.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And my thoughts at that time was I have a wife.
And my son was born the 1st of May 1946.
I had to provide a living for them.
I had to work.
And that was my aim, to make up for the five and a half years that I had spent during the war and to make a home, achieve something for myself.
And as the years went by, I did that.
Well, the first trade, of course, was the one that I left in '39, which was back to tailoring.
But that wasn't for me.
That was definitely not for me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had developed some kind of managerial capacity.
And I took a job as a salesman at a furniture store.
I could talk to people.
And I changed a couple of times.
Then I drifted back into the clothing industry.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I became a salesman.
I traveled.
I went to night school.
I studied.
When I left school at 14, I was very much an under-average scholar.
But life teaches you a great deal.
And I progressed.
I can remember highlights.
I mean, in five and a half years, you can-- an awful lot has happened.
There were some very-- there were some times when, yes, life is extremely difficult.
There were times when my life was in danger.
Perhaps not so dangerous as other men who fought in the services.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yes, I belonged to the Association of Jewish Ex-servicemen and Women.
And we have meetings.
And we have gatherings.
And we talk about the past.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We perpetuate the memories of our colleagues who never came back.
And even until today, we still are active as Jewish ex-servicemen and women.
We have a memorial room in East London-- both of the 1418 wars and the '39 wars.
We have some very heroic men of our religion.
We have members of the Victoria Cross, who earned very high gallantry awards.
And we still have annual remembrance parades in Whitehall, which as the years go by, of course, unfortunately become less and less, which we are very concerned about.
But we do make every attempt to make sure that what the Jewish people did during the war was of significant contribution.
And, currently, we are embarking on an education program to educate the young people of today to remember, not to forget.
All right.
